questions.question: The master gland of the body is:
Insulin is produced by which cells of the pancreas?
Goitre is caused by:

options.B: Deficiency of iodine causing thyroid enlargement

questions.question: ADH (Antidiuretic hormone) is produced in the hypothalamus and released by:
Glucagon increases blood glucose by:

options.B: Stimulating glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis in liver

questions.question: Cushing's syndrome is caused by:
The diagram shows the hypothalamo-pituitary axis. Which type of feedback inhibits TRH/TSH when T3/T4 are high?
questions.diagram_description: Diagram of HPT axis: Hypothalamus secretes TRH (Thyrotropin-releasing hormone) → Anterior pituitary secretes TSH (Thyroid-stimulating hormone) → Thyroid gland secretes T3 and T4. Negative feedback arrows shown: T3/T4 inhibiting both hypothalamus (long-loop) and anterior pituitary (short-loop). When T3/T4 high → less TRH and TSH → thyroid produces less hormone

questions.question: Epinephrine (adrenaline) is secreted by:
Dwarfism in children is caused by:
Parathyroid hormone (PTH) increases blood calcium by:

options.B: Activating osteoclasts and increasing Ca2+ reabsorption in kidneys

questions.question: The diagram shows the structure of the adrenal gland. Which zone of the cortex produces cortisol?
questions.diagram_description: Cross-section of adrenal gland showing outer cortex with three zones (from outside in): Zona glomerulosa (produces mineralocorticoids – aldosterone), Zona fasciculata (large, lipid-filled cells producing glucocorticoids – cortisol, the thickest zone), Zona reticularis (produces androgens – DHEA), and inner Adrenal medulla (chromaffin cells producing catecholamines – epinephrine/norepinephrine)

questions.question: Type 1 diabetes mellitus is caused by:
Melatonin is secreted by the:
The hormone that stimulates ovulation is:
Cretinism is caused by:

options.B: Thyroid hormone deficiency in infants leading to mental retardation

questions.question: The diagram shows hormone action via intracellular receptors. Which type of hormone uses this mechanism?
questions.diagram_description: Two mechanisms shown: (A) Peptide/protein hormone – binds cell surface receptor → second messenger (cAMP via adenylyl cyclase) → protein kinase cascade → cellular response; (B) Steroid/thyroid hormone – lipid soluble, crosses plasma membrane, binds intracellular receptor in cytoplasm or nucleus, hormone-receptor complex binds DNA at HRE → gene transcription → protein synthesis → cellular response

questions.question: Addison's disease is caused by:

options.B: Deficiency of adrenal cortex hormones (cortisol and aldosterone)

questions.question: Oxytocin is an example of:
Growth hormone (GH) excess in adults causes:
Prolactin is responsible for:
The diagram shows blood glucose regulation. In diabetes mellitus Type 2, what is the primary defect?
questions.diagram_description: Graph showing blood glucose after meal: normal curve (rises then falls back to 80-100mg/dL by 2hr), Type 1 DM (very high, no insulin secretion, no decrease), Type 2 DM (high, stays elevated longer – insulin produced but cells resistant). Feedback loop: high glucose → beta cells → insulin → cells take up glucose → glucose decreases → less insulin. In T2DM: receptor defect on target cells

options.B: Insulin resistance in target cells (receptors dysfunctional)

questions.question: The anterior pituitary is controlled by:

options.B: Releasing and inhibiting hormones from hypothalamus via portal blood

questions.question: Thyroid hormones (T3, T4) require which mineral for synthesis?
Aldosterone primarily acts on:
Hyperthyroidism (Graves' disease) symptoms include:

options.B: Weight loss, heat intolerance, tachycardia, exophthalmos

questions.question: The diagram shows the negative feedback in cortisol regulation. What happens when cortisol levels rise?
questions.diagram_description: Flow diagram: Stress → Hypothalamus releases CRH → Anterior pituitary releases ACTH → Adrenal cortex releases Cortisol. Negative feedback: High cortisol inhibits both hypothalamus (reduces CRH) and anterior pituitary (reduces ACTH sensitivity). Net result: cortisol falls back to normal level. Second loop shown for primary adrenal insufficiency (low cortisol → high CRH, ACTH)

questions.question: Second messenger in hormonal action via G-protein coupled receptors is:
Testosterone is produced by:
Gigantism occurs when excess GH is secreted:

options.B: In children before epiphyseal plate closure

questions.question: Somatostatin inhibits secretion of:

options.B: Both insulin and glucagon from pancreatic delta cells

questions.question: The diagram shows the location of endocrine glands. Which gland is located at the base of the brain attached to hypothalamus?
questions.diagram_description: Human body diagram with endocrine glands labeled: Brain with pineal gland (dorsal) and pituitary gland (ventral, attached to hypothalamus by infundibulum stalk), thyroid and parathyroids (neck), thymus (chest), adrenal glands (on top of kidneys), pancreas (abdomen, behind stomach), ovaries (female pelvis) or testes (male)

questions.question: Erythropoietin from kidneys stimulates:
Triiodothyronine (T3) is more potent than T4 because:

options.B: T3 has higher affinity for thyroid hormone receptors

questions.question: Atrial natriuretic peptide (ANP) is released when:

options.B: Blood volume/pressure increases, causing Na+ and water excretion

questions.question: Which of the following is a tropic hormone?
Hypothyroidism in adults causes:

options.B: Myxoedema – weight gain, cold intolerance, dry skin, bradycardia

questions.question: The islets of Langerhans are found in:
Which hormone opposes the action of insulin?
The diagram shows the effects of insulin vs glucagon on blood glucose homeostasis. Which hormone is secreted when blood glucose falls below 80 mg/dL?
questions.diagram_description: Blood glucose homeostasis diagram: Blood glucose rises after meal → beta cells secrete insulin → glucose uptake by liver (glycogenesis), muscle, adipose → blood glucose falls to normal (80–100mg/dL). Blood glucose falls (fasting/exercise) → alpha cells secrete glucagon → liver glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis → glucose released → blood glucose rises to normal. Set point shown

questions.question: The zona glomerulosa of adrenal cortex produces:
Pheochromocytoma is a tumor that causes excess secretion of:
FSH in males stimulates:
Diabetes insipidus is treated with:
The mechanism by which steroid hormones act involves:

options.A: Cell surface receptor and second messenger
options.B: Intracellular receptor → DNA binding → gene transcription

questions.question: Hypocalcaemia due to PTH deficiency causes:
The diagram shows the menstrual cycle hormone levels. Which hormone surge triggers ovulation?
questions.diagram_description: Graph of female reproductive cycle showing: FSH levels (rises early follicular phase, slight rise at ovulation), LH levels (major mid-cycle LH surge on day 14 triggering ovulation), Estrogen (gradual rise in follicular phase, peak just before ovulation, second rise in luteal phase), Progesterone (low in follicular phase, high in luteal phase after ovulation). Ovulation marked on day 14

questions.question: Which gland secretes thymosin hormone?
Conn's syndrome is characterized by:

options.B: Primary hyperaldosteronism causing hypertension and hypokalaemia

questions.question: ACTH (Adrenocorticotropic hormone) is released by:

options.B: Anterior pituitary (in response to CRH from hypothalamus)

questions.question: The hormone responsible for 'fight or flight' response is:
